skill.name: architecture-patterns
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants through organizational architecture decisions for Next.js, TypeScript, APIs, databases, state management, and React 19 patterns.
skill.summary: Choose consistent architecture patterns before implementing or reviewing frontend and full-stack features.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, path traversal, network, and environment access patterns. Manual review found these are markdown examples, internal API examples, or relative documentation links, with no executable files and only Read/Grep tool permissions. One React example builds a query URL without explicit encoding, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish with a minor documentation warning.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The reported backtick and command patterns occur inside markdown code fences or inline references. The skill contains no runnable script files, and SKILL.md limits tools to Read and Grep.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is markdown guidance or fenced sample code, not executable skill logic. The allowed tools do not include shell execution.

low_findings.title: Path traversal findings are relative documentation links
low_findings.description: The ../ sequences are links to adjacent skill documentation, not filesystem reads or writes. No evidence found of dynamic path construction or access to sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are markdown links and see-also references. There is no code path that consumes user input as a filesystem path.

low_findings.title: Network and environment findings are internal application examples
low_findings.description: Fetch calls target same-origin API routes, and JWT_SECRET appears in an authentication example. No evidence found of external exfiltration, credential collection, or unauthorized network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use local API paths and a normal server-side secret verification pattern. The content is still instructional, so confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Unencoded query parameter in React example
low_findings.description: A React 19 client action example interpolates form input directly into a search query URL. This is a minor documentation risk because generated implementations should encode query values before building URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly embeds a form value in a query string. The impact is limited because it is same-origin instructional code, not executable marketplace logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unencoded URL query interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: The search example should encode user input before adding it to a query string.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct interpolation of form input into a URL is visible in the example, although the example uses a same-origin endpoint.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when each feature uses different framework and data patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical index for choosing consistent implementation approaches.
actual_capabilities: Selects Next.js server, client, route handler, streaming, and server action patterns.
Guides TypeScript strict mode decisions using type guards, explicit types, and utility types.
Recommends Drizzle ORM query, relation, transaction, and migration patterns.
Maps state needs to React Query, useState, Context, URL state, or form state.
Outlines API route structure with validation, authentication, authorization, and response handling.
Summarizes React 19 patterns such as use(), useOptimistic, useFormStatus, and useActionState.
limitations: Provides architecture guidance, not a complete implementation for every referenced framework.
References adjacent skills that may not exist in every installation.
Does not run tests, inspect live services, or verify dependencies by itself.
Some examples are simplified and need project-specific security hardening.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Feature
use_cases.description: Choose data fetching, state, API, and validation patterns before coding a full-stack feature.

use_cases.title: Review Pattern Consistency
use_cases.description: Check whether a pull request follows the team standards for Next.js, TypeScript, and APIs.

use_cases.title: Onboard to Local Standards
use_cases.description: Learn the expected architecture choices without reading every detailed specialist skill first.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture-patterns skill to choose the best pattern for this feature: [describe feature]. Explain the recommended approach and why.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Approach
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture-patterns skill to review this implementation plan for Next.js, TypeScript, data access, state, and API choices: [paste plan].

prompt_templates.title: Design an API Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture-patterns skill to design an API flow for [resource or workflow]. Include validation, authentication, authorization, errors, and response shape.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture Drift
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture-patterns skill to audit these files for pattern drift. Identify mismatches, risks, and concrete changes: [paste file list or summaries].

output_examples.input: I need a dashboard that shows project data and supports deleting a project.
output: Use a Server Component for initial project data.
Use a Client Component for the delete interaction.
Use React Query if the list must refetch after mutation.
Validate and authorize deletion in the API route.

output_examples.input: Review this API route plan for creating users.
output: Add a Zod schema before business logic.
Return clear validation and authorization errors.
Avoid logging secrets or raw request bodies.

output_examples.input: Which state pattern should handle filters and pagination?
output: Use URL state with search parameters.
Keep the filter values shareable and bookmarkable.
Use local state only for temporary control state.

best_practices: Start with the decision tree before choosing a detailed implementation pattern.
Prefer server-side data fetching unless client interactivity requires another pattern.
Pair every API pattern with validation, authentication, and authorization checks.
anti_patterns: Using client-side fetching when a Server Component can load the same data.
Skipping TypeScript type guards for unknown external input.
Copying simplified examples without adapting security controls to the project.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps choose architecture patterns for Next.js, TypeScript, APIs, databases, state management, and React 19.

faq.question: Does it write complete production code?
faq.answer: It provides pattern guidance and examples. Production code still needs project-specific implementation and review.

faq.question: Can it help with code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare planned or existing code against the documented architecture choices.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require shell access?
faq.answer: No. The skill declares Read and Grep only, and the content is documentation-focused.

faq.question: What should users verify before publishing code from examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify validation, authorization, error handling, secret handling, and URL encoding in their own codebase.
